html:
Tesec Corporation (TSE:6337) | ¥2,519

Tesec is not the HBM burn-in monopoly implied by the old draft. The better thesis is cleaner: a tiny Japanese power-semiconductor tester and back-end handler supplier is being valued like a tired EV-cycle equipment name just as its handler orders are recovering on AI-related back-end demand, while roughly two-thirds of the equity value is covered by cash, securities and listed investments.

At ¥2,519, Yahoo Japan shows a 0.93x P/B, 3.97% dividend yield, and FY3/27 P/E around 24x. That P/E looks mediocre, but it is the wrong anchor. FY3/27 is an investment year, with Tesec guiding to only ¥450 million operating profit while raising R&D to a record ¥620 million. The company’s rolling plan is the real fulcrum: revenue recovers from ¥5.57 billion in FY3/26 to ¥8.0 billion in FY3/28 and ¥9.0 billion in FY3/29, with operating profit rising to ¥1.3 billion and then ¥1.85 billion as handler strength continues and testers recover.

The balance sheet makes the cyclicality tolerable. As of March 2026, Tesec had ¥3.50 billion of cash, ¥1.73 billion of securities and ¥4.02 billion of investment securities, against only about ¥260 million of borrowings in its FY3/26 results. Using 5.214 million shares excluding treasury, the economic market cap is about ¥13.1 billion and net financial assets are about ¥9.0 billion, or roughly ¥1,725 per share. That leaves the operating business valued at only about ¥4.1 billion. If management’s FY3/29 operating profit target of ¥1.85 billion is even roughly right, the operating company trades at barely 2.2x forward-cycle operating profit.

The misunderstood transition is inside the product mix. FY3/26 revenue fell 5.5%, but handler sales rose 53.9% to ¥2.55 billion while tester sales fell 34.8% to ¥1.97 billion. Total orders rose 35.1%, and Tesec says handler orders moved into recovery from Q3 after more than two years of weakness, helped by AI demand, while tester demand stayed depressed because of EV market adjustment in its May 2026 presentation. That is exactly the kind of split that creates a wrong label: investors see EV weakness and low current margins, but the order turn is coming first through back-end handlers in Malaysia and the Philippines, not through legacy power-device testers.

The macro setup supports that shift. SEMI expects back-end equipment growth to continue, with test equipment sales up 12.0% in 2026 and 7.1% in 2027, plus assembly and packaging equipment up 9.2% and 6.9%. Malaysia is also trying to move further into advanced packaging as AI reshapes the semiconductor chain. Tesec is small enough that a few Southeast Asian OSAT or captive back-end programs can matter. Its new 5880-IH strip-frame handler, with high/low temperature testing, 64 standard test sites and up to 255 optional sites, directly addresses higher-throughput back-end testing rather than generic commodity assembly.

Capital allocation has also improved enough that the cash should not be dismissed as dead money. Tesec paid a ¥100 dividend for FY3/26 and guides to another ¥100 for FY3/27, now split into interim and year-end payments. It bought back ¥599 million of stock in FY3/26, set a January 2026 buyback for up to 160,000 shares and ¥300 million, and will acquire 55,000 shares for free from a former president on May 26. It also sold one listed investment security for a ¥181 million gain to be recognized in Q1 FY3/27. This is still a conservative Japanese microcap, but it is no longer doing nothing with the balance sheet.

The current selloff is useful. The stock hit a 2026 high of ¥3,265 on May 12 and closed today, May 18, at ¥2,519 after investors digested weak near-term tester guidance. Retail sentiment is visible but not institutionally crowded: Yahoo Finance shows 83.33% “strong buy” board sentiment, yet today’s turnover was only ¥55.7 million. This is a tiny, illiquid Standard Market name with a semicap story that does not screen cleanly. That is where the opportunity is.

The base case is not heroic. Assume FY3/28 reaches ¥8.0 billion sales and ¥1.3 billion operating profit, then FY3/29 reaches something near the ¥9.0 billion and ¥1.85 billion plan. Give the operating business 7.5x FY3/29 operating profit, add about ¥9.0 billion of net financial assets, and the equity is worth roughly ¥4,500 per share. That is not a growth-stock multiple; it is a modest cyclical equipment multiple on a balance-sheet-backed company with a rising dividend and live buyback optionality.

The bull case requires the handler upcycle to be more structural: AI back-end packaging and power-management demand keep Southeast Asian customers ordering, the new handler platform wins share, tester demand rebounds from EV digestion, and revenue moves toward ¥10.5 billion to ¥11.0 billion with 20% plus operating margins. At 9x to 10x operating profit plus the financial asset base, and assuming some buybacks, ¥7,500 is plausible.

The bear case is that tester weakness persists, AI handler orders were a short replenishment cycle, R&D consumes margin, and management lets the securities portfolio sit while the market keeps applying a sub-1x P/B discount. Even then, the balance sheet and dividend yield matter. A 0.70x book valuation on roughly ¥2,700-plus BPS gives a bear price around ¥1,900.

Targets from ¥2,519: bear ¥1,900, down 25%; base ¥4,500, up 79%; bull ¥7,500, up 198%.
